steps.title: Identify the key facts about the eccentric moon
steps.content: The passage states: (1) three moons have prograde orbits; (2) "all but one of the 20 moons are thought to be remnants" of bodies broken apart in collisions; (3) the one exceptional moon orbits in the same direction as the planet's spin (prograde) but has a highly eccentric orbit, suggesting a different origin than the other 19.

steps.title: Evaluate each choice
steps.content:
• Choice A — doesn't have a retrograde orbit, but likely has the same origin as retrograde moons: The passage says the eccentric moon's origin may be different from all other 19 moons, not the same as the retrograde ones. ✗
  • Choice B — orbit is so tilted it's neither prograde nor retrograde: The passage explicitly says the moon orbits in the same direction as the planet's spin, i.e., it is prograde. ✗
  • Choice C — prograde orbit likely caused by a collision with another body: The passage says the eccentric orbit suggests a different origin than the other moons, which are the collision remnants; ascribing the orbit to a collision contradicts this. ✗
  • Choice D — prograde orbit and may not be a remnant of an earlier body orbiting Saturn: Correctly combines both facts: the orbit is prograde (same direction as planet's spin) and as the one exception to "all but one are remnants," it may not be a collision remnant. ✓
That matches choice D — D is correct.
